annotations.label: Binary tree

anchor.snippet: a binary tree is a tree data structure in which each node has at most two children

annotations.note: `Tree α` is the inductive type of binary trees with two children at each node, matching the Wikipedia notion.

annotations.label: Recursive set-theoretic definition of binary tree

anchor.snippet: A recursive definition using set theory is that a binary tree is a triple

annotations.note: The inductive `Tree.nil | Tree.node a l r` is precisely the recursive constructor presentation of a binary tree.

annotations.label: Full binary tree (recursive)

anchor.snippet: There exists a full tree consisting of a single node

annotations.note: No definition of `FullBinaryTree` (every node has 0 or 2 children) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Extended binary tree (recursive)

anchor.snippet: An empty set of nodes is an extended binary tree

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Tree` (with `nil` as external/empty node and `node` carrying two children) is precisely the extended binary tree.

annotations.label: Binary tree via graph theory

anchor.snippet: A binary tree is a rooted tree that is also an ordered tree

annotations.note: Mathlib has `SimpleGraph` arborescences but no graph-theoretic definition of a binary tree as a rooted ordered tree with branching ≤ 2.

annotations.label: Binary tree as edge-partitioned triplet

anchor.snippet: defining the binary tree as triplet

annotations.note: No formalization of a binary tree as a triple (V, E_left, E_right) of edge partitions exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Rooted binary tree

anchor.snippet: A rooted binary tree has a root node and every node has at most two children

annotations.note: `Tree α` is rooted with at most two children per node, which is the Wikipedia rooted binary tree.

annotations.label: Full binary tree

anchor.snippet: A full binary tree (sometimes referred to as a proper

annotations.note: No predicate `IsFull`/`Tree.full` for binary trees with all internal nodes of degree 2 is present.

annotations.label: Perfect binary tree

anchor.snippet: A perfect binary tree is a binary tree in which all interior nodes have two children

annotations.note: No definition of a perfect binary tree (all leaves at same depth) exists in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Complete binary tree

anchor.snippet: A complete binary tree is a binary tree in which every level

annotations.note: No notion of complete binary tree (filled levels, leaves leftmost on last level) appears in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Infinite complete binary tree

anchor.snippet: The infinite complete binary tree is a tree with

annotations.note: No formalization of the infinite complete binary tree (e.g., as `List Bool`) is provided as a named structure in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Balanced binary tree

anchor.snippet: A balanced binary tree is a binary tree structure in which the left and right subtrees

annotations.note: No height-balance predicate (AVL-style) on `Tree` is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Degenerate (pathological) tree

anchor.snippet: A degenerate (or pathological ) tree is where each parent node has only one associated child node

annotations.note: No predicate for degenerate/path-like binary trees is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bounds on number of nodes in a full binary tree

anchor.snippet: The number of nodes n in a full binary tree is at least

annotations.note: Bounds 2h+1 ≤ n ≤ 2^(h+1)−1 for full binary trees are not stated since `IsFull` itself isn't defined.

annotations.label: Number of leaves in a perfect binary tree

anchor.snippet: The number of leaf nodes l in a perfect binary tree is

annotations.note: Formula l = (n+1)/2 for perfect binary trees is absent (no perfect-tree predicate).

annotations.label: Leaves vs. degree-2 nodes relation

anchor.snippet: For any non-empty binary tree with

annotations.note: `numLeaves = numNodes + 1` is exactly the n0 = n2 + 1 identity under Mathlib's extended-tree convention where every `node` has two children and `nil` is a leaf.

annotations.label: Minimum tree height for n nodes

anchor.snippet: With given n nodes, the minimum possible tree height

annotations.note: Only the upper bound `height ≤ numNodes` is provided; no lower bound `height ≥ ⌈log₂(n+1)⌉` is formalized.

annotations.label: Minimum height for l leaves

anchor.snippet: A binary tree with l leaves has at least the height

annotations.note: No statement `height ≥ ⌈log₂ l⌉` (or `≥ l-1` for path trees) for binary trees was found.

annotations.label: Edges versus nodes in a binary tree

anchor.snippet: In a non-empty binary tree, if n is the total number of nodes and e is the total number of edges

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Tree` has no explicit edge-counting function, so the relation `e = n − 1` is not stated.

annotations.label: Number of null links

anchor.snippet: The number of null links (i.e., absent children of the nodes) in a binary tree of n nodes

annotations.note: Null links correspond to `nil` leaves in the extended representation, and `numLeaves = numNodes + 1` gives n + 1 null links.

annotations.label: Internal nodes in a complete binary tree

anchor.snippet: The number of internal nodes in a complete binary tree of n nodes

annotations.note: Without a complete-binary-tree definition, the ⌊n/2⌋ internal-node count is not formalized.

annotations.label: Parenthesizing a string for n = 3

anchor.snippet: For instance for n = 3 one has to parenthesize a string

annotations.note: No worked example enumerating the five parenthesizations for n = 3 is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Counting binary trees yields Catalan numbers

anchor.snippet: There is a unique binary tree of size 0

annotations.note: `#(treesOfNumNodesEq n) = catalan n` is the precise statement that binary trees with n internal nodes are counted by Catalan numbers.

annotations.label: Dyck words counted by Catalan numbers

anchor.snippet: The above parenthesized strings should not be confused with the set of words of length

annotations.note: States that Dyck words of semilength n are counted by `catalan n`.

annotations.label: Five Dyck words of length 6

anchor.snippet: So there are also five Dyck words of length 6

annotations.note: No explicit enumeration of the five length-6 Dyck words is given in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bijection between Dyck words and binary trees

anchor.snippet: These Dyck words do not correspond to binary trees in the same way

annotations.note: `DyckWord.equivTree : DyckWord ≃ Tree Unit` is exactly this bijection.

annotations.label: Binary trees as free magma on a singleton

anchor.snippet: The ability to represent binary trees as strings of symbols and parentheses implies that binary trees can represent the elements of a free magma on a singleton set

annotations.note: `FreeMagma α` exists with the comment that it can be viewed as labelled binary trees, but no explicit equivalence `FreeMagma Unit ≃ Tree Unit` (without `nil`) is proved.

annotations.label: Array storage of binary trees

anchor.snippet: Binary trees can also be stored in breadth-first order as an implicit data structure in arrays

annotations.note: No array/implicit heap-style representation of `Tree` is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Succinct binary tree encoding

anchor.snippet: A succinct data structure is one which occupies close to minimum possible space

annotations.note: Succinct data structures are not part of Mathlib.

annotations.label: Information-theoretic bound for binary tree encoding

anchor.snippet: The number of different binary trees on

annotations.note: The 2n − Θ(log n) lower bound on bits needed to encode binary trees is not formalized.

annotations.label: Preorder succinct representation

anchor.snippet: One simple representation which meets this bound is to visit the nodes of the tree in preorder

annotations.note: No preorder bitstring encoding of `Tree` is provided in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Left-child right-sibling correspondence

anchor.section: Encoding ordered trees as binary trees
anchor.snippet: There is a natural one-to-one correspondence between ordered trees and binary trees

annotations.note: No left-child/right-sibling bijection between ordered (rose) trees and binary trees is formalized.

annotations.label: Leaf node insertion

anchor.snippet: To add a new node after leaf node A

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Tree` API has no insertion-at-leaf operation.

annotations.label: Internal node insertion

anchor.snippet: Insertion on internal nodes is slightly more complex than on leaf nodes

annotations.note: No internal-node insertion operation is provided for `Tree`.

annotations.label: Deletion of node with zero or one children

anchor.section: Node with zero or one children
anchor.snippet: Suppose that the node to delete is node A

annotations.note: No deletion operation for `Tree` nodes is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Ambiguity of deleting node with two children

anchor.snippet: In a binary tree, a node with two children cannot be deleted unambiguously

annotations.note: No statement/lemma about ambiguity of deletion in binary trees is in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Pre-order traversal

anchor.snippet: In pre-order, we always visit the current node

annotations.note: `Tree.traverse` visits node, then left, then right subtree (i.e. pre-order applicative traversal), but no named `Tree.preorderList`/`toListPreorder` function exists.

annotations.label: In-order traversal

anchor.snippet: In in-order, we always recursively traverse the current node's left subtree

annotations.note: No in-order traversal of `Tree` is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Post-order traversal

anchor.snippet: In post-order, we always recursively traverse the current node's left subtree

annotations.note: No post-order traversal of `Tree` is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Depth-first order

anchor.snippet: In depth-first order, we always attempt to visit the node farthest from the root node

annotations.note: No DFS algorithm on `Tree` is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Breadth-first order

anchor.snippet: Contrasting with depth-first order is breadth-first order

annotations.note: No BFS traversal on `Tree` is formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Breadth-index traversal in complete binary tree

anchor.snippet: In a complete binary tree, a node's breadth-index

annotations.note: No relation between breadth-index and traversal directions in a complete binary tree is formalized.
